source.title: Lifecycle Growth Experiments Sprint (4 weeks) — Sprint Brief
source.author: Ben Carter
source.content:
## Summary
This sprint is a 4-week, cross-functional Growth + Product effort to improve self-serve activation and early retention for the **Hosted API**.

We will run a tightly-instrumented set of experiments across:
- **Lifecycle email:** onboarding drip v2
- **Console:** contextual onboarding nudges (feature-flagged)
- **Paid retargeting:** conservative audiences focused on re-engaging non-activated and early-activated users

This is a measurement-first sprint: we are prioritizing clean event taxonomy, holdouts, and decision criteria to avoid “noisy” learnings.

---

## Sprint goals (what success looks like)
### Primary activation goals
Within **7 days** of signup:
1) Increase **% of new signups completing a first successful request**
2) Increase **% of new signups reaching the first 100k tokens milestone**

### Secondary goals
- Reduce **time-to-first-token (TTFT)** and “time-to-value” (time from signup to first successful response)
- Improve **D7 and D30 active usage** for new cohorts
- Improve downstream conversion signals (upgrade intent / sales-assisted handoff when appropriate)

### Decision outcomes required by end of Week 4
For each experiment, produce a clear **keep / iterate / kill** recommendation, plus the follow-on backlog.

---

## KPI definitions (operational)
**Activation funnel milestones** (all measured at the user + org level):
- **Signup**: account created event (deduped)
- **API key created**: first API key created via Console or API
- **First successful request**: first 2xx response from Hosted API generation/chat/embeddings endpoints (excluding health checks)
- **First 100k tokens**: cumulative tokens served for the org reaches 100k

**Primary metrics**
- **Activation-1 (A1)**: % of new signups with first successful request within 7 days
- **Activation-2 (A2)**: % of new signups reaching first 100k tokens within 7 days

**Secondary metrics**
- **Median time to first successful request** (and P75)
- **Median TTFT** for first request (and P75)
- **D7 active**: org has any Hosted API usage on day 7 (local time) after signup
- **D30 active**: org has any Hosted API usage on day 30 after signup

**Reporting notes**
- We will use cohort-based reporting by signup date; all funnel steps must use the standardized lifecycle event taxonomy and dedupe rules.
- We will always report overall + segmented by: region, SDK vs raw HTTP, use-case endpoint family (chat vs embeddings), and first-day token volume bucket.

---

## Hypotheses (what we believe)
1) **Reducing “first request” friction** will drive a measurable lift in A1 and reduce time-to-value.
2) **Contextual next-step guidance in Console** will reduce drop-off between API key creation → first request.
3) **Timely, targeted lifecycle email** will re-engage stalled signups (no API key / no first request) without harming deliverability or support load.
4) **Conservative retargeting** can bring back signup-no-activation users and activated-but-no-return users at acceptable CPA, without interfering with active enterprise opportunities.

---

## Scope
### In scope
- Email onboarding drip v2: copy, timing, trigger logic, segmentation, compliance hooks
- Console nudges: placements, targeting rules, feature flags, dismissal persistence, UX/copy iteration
- Retargeting: audience definitions, creative briefs, UTMs, spend caps, exclusions for enterprise opps
- Measurement: event taxonomy, dashboards, experiment design, weekly readouts

### Out of scope
- Major product re-architecture
- Pricing/packaging changes
- Large-scale docs replatforming (docs updates limited to onboarding alignment and quickstart fixes)

---

## Experiment principles
- **Holdout by default**: where possible, maintain a clean holdout group per channel.
- **No overlapping interventions without intent**: avoid sending multiple conflicting nudges/emails to the same cohort without explicitly testing the combination.
- **Guardrails are enforced**: deliverability and compliance thresholds can pause experiments.
- **Bias for fast iteration**: ship v1 quickly, learn, then iterate within sprint constraints.

---

## Experiments (planned)
The following experiments are the planned “spine” of the sprint. Details live in the linked design specs.

### Experiment list (overview table)
| ID | Channel | Hypothesis | Target cohort | Variants | Primary metric | Duration | Owner |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| E1 | Email | Triggered onboarding emails reduce stalled signup drop-off | New signups (self-serve) | Control vs Drip v2 | A1 within 7d | 7–10 days (rolling) | Ben Carter |
| E2 | Email | Segmented “stalled” sequences outperform generic onboarding | No API key at +2h; No first request at +24h | Timing/CTA variant A vs B | A1 within 7d + time-to-value | 7–10 days | Ben Carter |
| C1 | Console nudge | “Create API key” nudge reduces signup → key drop-off | Signed in; no API key | Nudge vs holdout | API key creation rate | 5–7 days | Marissa Cole |
| C2 | Console nudge | “Run first request” nudge increases first successful request | Has API key; no successful request | Nudge vs holdout | A1 within 7d | 5–7 days | Marissa Cole |
| C3 | Console nudge | Streaming guidance accelerates TTFT and perceived speed | First-time requesters | Nudge vs none | time-to-value + TTFT (P75) | 7 days | Marissa Cole |
| R1 | Retargeting | Retargeting recovers signup-no-activation at acceptable CPA | Signup-no-activation (7d lookback) | Creative set A vs B | A1 within 7d + CPA | 10–14 days | Tyler Benson |
| R2 | Retargeting | Retargeting activated-but-no-return increases D7 activity | Activated; inactive for 3–7 days | Creative set A vs B | D7 active + CPA | 10–14 days | Tyler Benson |

Notes:
- Email and Console experiments must share consistent event definitions and cohort IDs for attribution.
- Retargeting will use strict exclusions (see Guardrails) and conservative lookback windows.

---

## Measurement & instrumentation
### Event taxonomy and required properties
All lifecycle events must include, at minimum:
- user_id, org_id
- event_time (UTC)
- source (console | api | email)
- environment (prod)
- region
- experiment_cohort_id (if eligible)
- experiment_id + variant (when applicable)

### Funnel dashboard requirements
Dashboards must support:
- Funnel conversion rates (signup → key → first request → 100k tokens)
- Time-to-step distributions (median, P75)
- Breakdowns: region, acquisition source, SDK usage, endpoint family
- Alerts for sudden step regressions after experiment launches

### Attribution rules (high-level)
- Email: attribute to email variant if user was eligible and received the email prior to the relevant step.
- Console: attribute to nudge variant if user was eligible and saw the nudge prior to the relevant step.
- Retargeting: measure incremental lift using holdout where feasible; otherwise conservative interpretation with UTM-based directional signals.

---

## Guardrails (must not break)
### Deliverability (email)
Pause or ramp down if any threshold is exceeded (rolling 24–48h, Marketing Ops to validate):
- Complaint rate > **0.15%**
- Hard bounce rate > **1.0%**
- Unsubscribe rate > **1.5%** on any single send
- “Not receiving onboarding email” support tickets spike (qualitative escalation)

Mitigations:
- Throttle ramps by cohort size; warm-up domains; list hygiene checks
- Suppress re-sends for users who already activated (API key or first request)

### Compliance
- CAN-SPAM: functional unsubscribe in every lifecycle email
- GDPR: respect consent/opt-out; audience usage adheres to internal data handling policy
- Ensure preference updates propagate to all sending systems and are reflected in Console

### Retargeting safeguards
- Exclude:
  - Active enterprise opportunities (as defined by Sales leadership)
  - Sensitive/regulated accounts flagged by Sales/Compliance
  - Known high-intent inbound leads routed to sales-assisted flows (avoid conflicting messaging)
- Spend caps (initial): daily cap + total sprint cap per channel (approved by Exec Sponsor)

### Product/Support load
- If a nudge causes a functional regression (e.g., blocks API key creation), roll back within **2 hours** of confirmation.
- If support tickets for onboarding issues increase materially week-over-week, triage immediately with Console + Growth.

---

## Owners & operating cadence
### Core owners
- Experiment owner (overall): **Ben Carter**
- Executive sponsor: **Camila Reyes**
- Messaging review: **Soojin Lee**
- Console surface area: **Marissa Cole**
- Telemetry/rollout input: **Paula Mendes**
- Marketing ops & attribution: **Tyler Benson**
- Sales exclusions & handoff rules: **Jordan Blake**, **Stephanie Nguyen**
- Privacy/legal review: **Michael Grant**, **Olga Petrov**

### Weekly cadence
- Mon: instrument + launch checklist review; go/no-go decisions
- Mid-week: monitor guardrails and early signals (deliverability, CTR, funnel sanity)
- Fri: experiment readout and decisioning (keep/iterate/kill)

---

## Timeline (by week)
### Week 1 — Definitions + first email launch
Deliverables:
- Final KPI definitions + taxonomy alignment
- Dashboard v1 live with baseline cohort
- Email onboarding drip v2 copy finalized + compliance review
- Launch first email A/B (E1)

### Week 2 — Console nudge framework + first nudge
Deliverables:
- Nudge component + feature flag targeting + holdout mechanics
- QA pass on nudge placement and dismissal persistence
- Launch first nudge experiment (C1) and/or (C2)

### Week 3 — Retargeting launch + refine
Deliverables:
- Retargeting audiences live + exclusions validated with Sales
- Creatives shipped + landing pages mapped + UTMs standardized
- Spend/CPA monitoring; iterate creative/audiences as needed

### Week 4 — Consolidated analysis + recommendations
Deliverables:
- Consolidated readout with channel-by-channel and combined effects
- Recommendations and rollout plan for “always-on” wins
- Follow-on backlog with sizing + dependencies

---

## Launch checklist (minimum bar)
Before any experiment goes live:
1) Event emission verified in at least two regions; dedupe confirmed
2) Dashboard shows expected counts vs baseline (sanity check)
3) Holdout definition confirmed and documented
4) Guardrails monitored with named on-call owner (deliverability + funnel health)
5) Sales exclusions applied for retargeting audiences (signed off by Sales leadership)
6) Unsubscribe/preference handling validated end-to-end

---

## Risks & mitigations
- **Risk: Event tracking discrepancies skew results** → Mitigation: pre-launch sanity queries; compare API logs vs analytics; pause interpretation until resolved.
- **Risk: Email deliverability degradation** → Mitigation: conservative ramps, list hygiene, monitoring thresholds, escalation path to deliverability support.
- **Risk: Console nudge UX regressions** → Mitigation: feature flags + rapid rollback; QA for edge cases; monitor support.
- **Risk: Retargeting conflicts with enterprise motion** → Mitigation: strict exclusions and weekly sync with Sales; suppress by domain and active opp list.

---

## Open questions (tracked during Week 1)
- What is the default “time window” for considering a user “stalled” for each trigger (2h/24h/72h)?
- Which landing page performs best for retargeting: Console login, quickstart doc, or minimal “hello world” template?
- Do we treat “first successful request” as any endpoint or only chat/generation? (Default: any core inference endpoint; exclude health checks.)

---

## References
- Email onboarding drip v2 spec: /product-docs/design-specs/email-onboarding-drip-v2
- Console nudge framework: /product-docs/design-specs/console-onboarding-nudges-experiment-framework
- Activation/Retention theme (Q1 2026): /product-docs/roadmap-and-themes/q1-2026-activation-retention-theme
- Data handling policy (lifecycle + retargeting): /policies-and-process/data-management/lifecycle-email-retargeting-data-handling
- Messaging guidelines (Hosted API): /sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/onboarding-messaging-guidelines-hosted-api

- experiment_cohort_id (if eligible)
- experiment_id + variant (when applicable)

### Funnel dashboard requirements
Dashboards must support:
- Funnel conversion rates (signup → key → first request → 100k tokens)
- Time-to-step distributions (median, P75)
- Breakdowns: region, acquisition source, SDK usage, endpoint family
- Alerts for sudden step regressions after experiment launches

### Attribution rules (high-level)
- Email: attribute to email variant if user was eligible and received the email prior to the relevant step.
- Console: attribute to nudge variant if user was eligible and saw the nudge prior to the relevant step.
- Retargeting: measure incremental lift using holdout where feasible; otherwise conservative interpretation with UTM-based directional signals.

---

## Guardrails (must not break)
### Deliverability (email)
Pause or ramp down if any threshold is exceeded (rolling 24–48h, Marketing Ops to validate):
- Complaint rate > **0.15%**
- Hard bounce rate > **1.0%**
- Unsubscribe rate > **1.5%** on any single send
- “Not receiving onboarding email” support tickets spike (qualitative escalation)

Mitigations:
- Throttle ramps by cohort size; warm-up domains; list hygiene checks
- Suppress re-sends for users who already activated (API key or first request)

### Compliance
- CAN-SPAM: functional unsubscribe in every lifecycle email
- GDPR: respect consent/opt-out; audience usage adheres to internal data handling policy
- Ensure preference updates propagate to all sending systems and are reflected in Console

### Retargeting safeguards
- Exclude:
  - Active enterprise opportunities (as defined by Sales leadership)
  - Sensitive/regulated accounts flagged by Sales/Compliance
  - Known high-intent inbound leads routed to sales-assisted flows (avoid conflicting messaging)
- Spend caps (initial): daily cap + total sprint cap per channel (approved by Exec Sponsor)

### Product/Support load
- If a nudge causes a functional regression (e.g., blocks API key creation), roll back within **2 hours** of confirmation.
- If support tickets for onboarding issues increase materially week-over-week, triage immediately with Console + Growth.

---

## Owners & operating cadence
### Core owners
- Experiment owner (overall): **Ben Carter**
- Executive sponsor: **Camila Reyes**
- Messaging review: **Soojin Lee**
- Console surface area: **Marissa Cole**
- Telemetry/rollout input: **Paula Mendes**
- Marketing ops & attribution: **Tyler Benson**
- Sales exclusions & handoff rules: **Jordan Blake**, **Stephanie Nguyen**
- Privacy/legal review: **Michael Grant**, **Olga Petrov**

### Weekly cadence
- Mon: instrument + launch checklist review; go/no-go decisions
- Mid-week: monitor guardrails and early signals (deliverability, CTR, funnel sanity)
- Fri: experiment readout and decisioning (keep/iterate/kill)

---

## Timeline (by week)
### Week 1 — Definitions + first email launch
Deliverables:
- Final KPI definitions + taxonomy alignment
- Dashboard v1 live with baseline cohort
- Email onboarding drip v2 copy finalized + compliance review
- Launch first email A/B (E1)

### Week 2 — Console nudge framework + first nudge
Deliverables:
- Nudge component + feature flag targeting + holdout mechanics
- QA pass on nudge placement and dismissal persistence
- Launch first nudge experiment (C1) and/or (C2)

### Week 3 — Retargeting launch + refine
Deliverables:
- Retargeting audiences live + exclusions validated with Sales
- Creatives shipped + landing pages mapped + UTMs standardized
- Spend/CPA monitoring; iterate creative/audiences as needed

### Week 4 — Consolidated analysis + recommendations
Deliverables:
- Consolidated readout with channel-by-channel and combined effects
- Recommendations and rollout plan for “always-on” wins
- Follow-on backlog with sizing + dependencies

---

## Launch checklist (minimum bar)
Before any experiment goes live:
1) Event emission verified in at least two regions; dedupe confirmed
2) Dashboard shows expected counts vs baseline (sanity check)
3) Holdout definition confirmed and documented
4) Guardrails monitored with named on-call owner (deliverability + funnel health)
5) Sales exclusions applied for retargeting audiences (signed off by Sales leadership)
6) Unsubscribe/preference handling validated end-to-end

---

## Risks & mitigations
- **Risk: Event tracking discrepancies skew results** → Mitigation: pre-launch sanity queries; compare API logs vs analytics; pause interpretation until resolved.
- **Risk: Email deliverability degradation** → Mitigation: conservative ramps, list hygiene, monitoring thresholds, escalation path to deliverability support.
- **Risk: Console nudge UX regressions** → Mitigation: feature flags + rapid rollback; QA for edge cases; monitor support.
- **Risk: Retargeting conflicts with enterprise motion** → Mitigation: strict exclusions and weekly sync with Sales; suppress by domain and active opp list.

---

## Open questions (tracked during Week 1)
- What is the default “time window” for considering a user “stalled” for each trigger (2h/24h/72h)?
- Which landing page performs best for retargeting: Console login, quickstart doc, or minimal “hello world” template?
- Do we treat “first successful request” as any endpoint or only chat/generation? (Default: any core inference endpoint; exclude health checks.)

---

## References
- Email onboarding drip v2 spec: /product-docs/design-specs/email-onboarding-drip-v2
- Console nudge framework: /product-docs/design-specs/console-onboarding-nudges-experiment-framework
- Activation/Retention theme (Q1 2026): /product-docs/roadmap-and-themes/q1-2026-activation-retention-theme
- Data handling policy (lifecycle + retargeting): /policies-and-process/data-management/lifecycle-email-retargeting-data-handling
- Messaging guidelines (Hosted API): /sales-enablement/positioning-and-messaging/onboarding-messaging-guidelines-hosted-api

source.title: Email Onboarding Drip v2 — Design Spec (Hosted API Activation)
source.author: Ben Carter
source.body:
## Overview
Email Onboarding Drip v2 is the primary lifecycle email sequence for new self-serve Hosted API signups. The sequence is designed to reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT), increase early activation (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), and improve D7/D30 return usage.

This document specifies triggers, segmentation, send schedule, copy variants, compliance requirements, deliverability guardrails, and the measurement / experiment plan.

**Primary audience:** Developers who sign up for Redwood API self-serve and have not yet reached early activation milestones.

**Owners**
- Experiment owner: Ben Carter (Growth/Marketing)
- Messaging review: Soojin Lee
- Marketing ops + attribution: Tyler Benson
- Telemetry + rollout input: Paula Mendes
- Legal/compliance review: Michael Grant (CAN-SPAM), Olga Petrov (GDPR)

---

## Goals and success metrics
### Goals
1) Increase activation within 7 days:
- **Activation milestone A:** “First successful request” (any successful completion from SDK/REST).
- **Activation milestone B:** “First 100k tokens” (cumulative) within 7 days of signup.

2) Reduce TTFT:
- Decrease median time from signup → first successful request.

3) Improve early retention:
- Increase D7 active usage and reduce “one-and-done” behavior.

### Success metrics (email + product)
**Email engagement (diagnostic):**
- Delivered rate
- Open rate (directional; impacted by privacy features)
- Click-through rate (CTR)
- Click-to-open rate (CTOR)
- Complaint rate, unsubscribe rate

**Activation / usage outcomes (decision metrics):**
- Signup → API key created conversion
- Signup → first successful request conversion
- Signup → first 100k tokens within 7 days conversion
- Median TTFT
- D7 active usage rate

**Guardrails:**
- Complaint rate < 0.08% weekly
- Unsubscribe rate < 1.0% per send (monitor trend)
- Hard bounce rate < 0.5%
- Support tickets for “not receiving onboarding email” should not exceed baseline +20% without investigation

---

## Non-goals / out of scope
- Pricing/packaging changes
- Major docs replatforming
- Complex personalization beyond core lifecycle events
- Advanced multi-language localization (v2 is English-only; revisit in Q2)

---

## Segmentation and eligibility
### Base eligibility
Users are eligible if:
- They create a Redwood account via self-serve Hosted API signup
- They have not opted out of marketing/lifecycle communications
- They are not suppressed by compliance or sales exclusions

### Suppression / exclusions
Suppress sends for:
1) **Unsubscribed users** (global marketing opt-out and/or lifecycle opt-out).
2) **Transactional-only preference** (if user selects that preference in console).
3) **Active enterprise opportunities / sensitive accounts** (to avoid conflicting outreach):
   - Domains / accounts on the Sales exclusion list (maintained weekly with Sales leadership).
   - Accounts flagged “Sales-assisted onboarding in progress” in CRM.
4) **Regulated / restricted tracking accounts** where policy requires reduced targeting (per data handling policy; see related page).

### Lifecycle cohorts (v2)
We define cohorts based on onboarding progress.

| Cohort | Definition (evaluated at send time) | Primary objective |
|---|---|---|
| New signup | Signed up within last 24h | Get to API key + first request |
| Stalled: no API key | Signup ≥ 2h ago AND no API key created | Push key creation and quickstart |
| Stalled: API key but no request | API key created AND no successful request | Get a working “hello world” call |
| First success (early) | First successful request completed AND tokens < 100k | Encourage streaming + dashboard + scale up |

**Note:** Cohorts are mutually exclusive at evaluation time; users can “graduate” to later cohorts and stop receiving earlier-stage messages.

---

## Event dependencies (triggers and state)
Email eligibility and stage progression are driven by product events.

### Required events (canonical names)
- `signup_completed`
- `api_key_created`
- `first_successful_request` (first time)
- `tokens_100k_reached` (first time)
- `email_unsubscribed` / `email_preferences_updated`

### Required user attributes (minimum)
- `user_id` (internal)
- `workspace_id` (if applicable)
- `primary_email`
- `signup_timestamp`
- `region` (for compliance + deliverability monitoring)
- `utm_source`, `utm_campaign` (if present at signup)

### Dedupe rules
- `first_successful_request` and `tokens_100k_reached` must be emitted once per user/workspace (per analytics spec).
- Email sends are idempotent by `(user_id, email_template_id, stage_id)`.

---

## Trigger logic and send schedule
This is a staged drip with time-based triggers and event-based exits.

### High-level sequence
1) **Welcome + Quickstart** (T+15 min after signup)
2) **No API key reminder** (T+6h if no API key)
3) **Make your first request** (T+24h if API key but no first success)
4) **Streaming + reliability tips** (T+48h if still no first success OR low engagement)
5) **After first success: scale to 100k tokens** (event-based; T+2h after first success)
6) **D7 check-in** (T+7d after signup; conditional)

### Detailed schedule and gating
| Email | Template ID | Trigger | Send window | Exit condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1: Welcome + quickstart | `onboard_v2_e1` | `signup_completed` | 15 min after signup | Stop if user unsubscribes |
| E2: Create an API key | `onboard_v2_e2` | Time-based check | 6h after signup | Do not send if `api_key_created` already occurred |
| E3: First request (hello world) | `onboard_v2_e3` | Time-based check | 24h after signup | Do not send if `first_successful_request` already occurred |
| E4: Debug + common pitfalls | `onboard_v2_e4` | Time-based check | 48h after signup | Do not send if `first_successful_request` already occurred |
| E5: After first success | `onboard_v2_e5` | `first_successful_request` | 2h after first success | Do not send if `tokens_100k_reached` already occurred |
| E6: D7 check-in | `onboard_v2_e6` | Time-based check | 7d after signup | Suppress if user has been active in last 48h AND has reached 100k tokens |

### Quiet hours + throttling
- **Default send window:** 08:00–17:00 recipient local time when possible (fallback to account region).
- **Deliverability ramp:** Start at 10% of eligible users/day for 2 days, then 25%, then 50%, then 100% if guardrails hold.

---

## Messaging framework (what we emphasize)
We align with Hosted API onboarding messaging guidelines:
- **Developer-first:** clear steps, minimal jargon, copy that assumes “I just want my first request to work.”
- **Production outcomes:** low-latency inference, predictable cost, reliability/fallbacks, and easy path from prototype to production.
- **Avoid overpromising:** no absolute latency guarantees in lifecycle email; point to benchmarks/docs when needed.

Key proof points to weave in (lightweight):
- Streaming support
- Clear unit economics controls (batching/caching/quantization—only mention if relevant to stage)
- Observability (usage dashboard and token-level cost breakdown)

---

## Content structure and modules
Each email is assembled from standardized modules to keep design consistent and reduce iteration time.

**Shared modules**
- Header: Redwood Inference (simple, dark-on-light)
- One-sentence value prop (stage-specific)
- Primary CTA button
- Code block / snippet (where applicable)
- “Need help?” link (docs + support)
- Footer: address, unsubscribe, preferences

**Design constraints**
- Mobile-first; keep above-the-fold to a single CTA + 1–2 short paragraphs.
- Code blocks must wrap gracefully; use short lines and avoid wide tables.

---

## A/B test plan (v2 initial)
We will A/B test **E1** and **E3** first because they are highest-volume and closest to activation.

### Experiment: E1 subject line + hero emphasis
**Hypothesis:** Emphasizing speed-to-first-token and a single actionable step will increase click-through and improve first request conversion.

- **Control (A):** “Welcome to Redwood Inference — your API key is ready”
- **Variant (B):** “Make your first LLM request in 2 minutes”

Hero copy direction:
- A: “Get started with your API key and quickstart.”
- B: “Run a single command and see tokens stream back.”

Primary CTA:
- A: “View quickstart”
- B: “Send your first request”

### Experiment: E3 “hello world” CTA destination
**Hypothesis:** Sending users directly to a minimal copy-paste snippet (vs. broader docs) reduces TTFT and increases first success.

- **Control (A):** CTA → docs quickstart page
- **Variant (B):** CTA → minimal “first request” snippet page (dedicated landing section)

### Assignment and holdout
- Random assignment by `user_id` at signup.
- Persist variant assignment across the sequence.
- Optional: 5% global holdout (no onboarding drip) for measuring absolute lift vs. baseline. Holdout requires exec sponsor sign-off for duration.

---

## Copy outlines (per email)
Final copy lives in the copy variant doc; below are the v2 outlines and required elements.

### E1: Welcome + quickstart (T+15m)
**Objective:** get user to API key + first request as fast as possible.
- Subject (A/B; see test plan)
- Body outline:
  1) “You’re in. Here’s the fastest path to your first token.”
  2) 3-step checklist:
     - Create API key
     - Run the snippet (SDK or curl)
     - See a streaming response
  3) CTA button
  4) Secondary links: docs quickstart, example app template

**Required links:**
- Quickstart docs
- Minimal snippet
- Console API keys page

### E2: No API key reminder (T+6h; conditional)
**Objective:** remove friction for API key creation.
- Subject: “Create your API key to start sending requests”
- Body:
  - Short explanation of where to find keys
  - Security note: “Treat keys like passwords; rotate if exposed.”
  - CTA: “Create API key”

### E3: First request (T+24h; conditional)
**Objective:** get a successful request.
- Subject: “Your first request is one copy/paste away”
- Body:
  - Provide 15–25 line snippet (TypeScript by default; link to Python)
  - Call out common failure modes (missing key, wrong base URL)
  - CTA: (A/B destination)

### E4: Debug + common pitfalls (T+48h; conditional)
**Objective:** unblock stalled users.
- Subject: “Stuck? 3 quick fixes to get your first response”
- Body:
  - Troubleshooting bullets:
    1) API key not set / invalid
    2) Model name typo
    3) Network/VPC restrictions (if applicable)
  - CTA: “See troubleshooting guide”
  - Secondary: “Reply to this email / contact support” (if supported by tooling)

### E5: After first success (event-based)
**Objective:** move from first success to meaningful usage; introduce console value.
- Subject: “Nice — now make it fast and observable”
- Body:
  - Encourage streaming
  - Link to usage dashboard (token + latency breakdown)
  - CTA: “View usage dashboard”

### E6: D7 check-in (T+7d; conditional)
**Objective:** re-engage users who tried once and paused.
- Subject: “Want help getting Redwood into production?”
- Body:
  - Offer 2 paths:
    1) self-serve resources (examples, docs)
    2) talk to solutions (only if not excluded and showing upgrade intent)
  - CTA: “See production checklist”

---

## Compliance requirements (CAN-SPAM, GDPR)
### Required footer elements
- Physical mailing address
- Clear unsubscribe link (one-click)
- Link to email preferences center

### Preference handling
- Unsubscribe and preference changes must be respected within 24 hours (target: immediate).
- Preference state must sync between email platform and Redwood console preferences.

### GDPR considerations
- Purpose limitation: onboarding emails are permitted as product/lifecycle communication for new users, but targeting must follow the internal data handling policy.
- Data minimization: do not include sensitive attributes in email payloads.
- Right to opt out / right to be forgotten: preference center must be accessible, and suppression lists must honor deletions.

### Prohibited content / practices
- Do not imply that performance claims are guaranteed.
- Do not add users to retargeting audiences based on email engagement alone.
- Do not include any user-provided prompt content in emails.

---

## Deliverability and sending reputation
### Authentication / configuration (owned by Marketing Ops)
- SPF, DKIM, DMARC must pass.
- Dedicated sending domain recommended if volume increases (tracked separately).

### Monitoring (weekly)
- Bounce rate, complaint rate, unsub rate
- Spam placement sampling (if vendor supports)
- “Not receiving onboarding email” support reports

### Ramp plan
- New templates launch at reduced volume (10% → 25% → 50% → 100%) with daily checks.
- If complaint rate or hard bounce exceeds guardrail thresholds, pause expansion and investigate.

---

## Tracking, UTMs, and instrumentation
### UTM conventions
All links must include:
- `utm_source=lifecycle_email`
- `utm_medium=email`
- `utm_campaign=onboarding_v2`
- `utm_content={template_id}`
- `utm_term={variant_id}` (if A/B)

### Required email events
Captured in email platform and forwarded to analytics:
- delivered
- opened (directional)
- clicked (by link id)
- unsubscribed
- spam_complaint

### Product conversion attribution
Primary conversion events are product-side and should be measured independent of email tracking limitations:
- `api_key_created`
- `first_successful_request`
- `tokens_100k_reached`

We will attribute lift using cohort analysis and holdout/A-B comparisons, not last-click.

---

## Measurement plan and reporting
### Funnel views
We will report the onboarding funnel with breakdowns by:
- cohort (new/stalled/first success)
- variant (A/B)
- acquisition channel (UTM at signup)
- region

### Decision criteria
- A variant can “win” if it improves **first successful request within 7 days** by **≥ +3% relative** (or statistically credible lift) without violating deliverability guardrails.
- Secondary confirmation: reduction in median TTFT.

### Time windows
- Primary window: 7 days post-signup.
- Retention check: D7 and D30 (directional within sprint; more confidence post-sprint).

---

## Launch checklist
1) Copy + design approved (Soojin + Design)
2) Compliance review completed (Michael + Olga)
3) Preference center link validated in console
4) Trigger logic QA:
   - eligible / ineligible cases
   - stage exits working (first success should stop E3/E4)
5) UTM link audit
6) Test sends across Gmail/Outlook + mobile
7) Ramp plan configured + monitoring owner on-call for first 72 hours
8) Dashboard sanity checks (baseline vs. post-launch)

---

## Open questions / follow-ups
- Do we enable “reply-to monitored inbox” for E4 troubleshooting, or route to support form only?
- Should E6 include a lightweight “book a 15-min onboarding call” CTA, gated by upgrade intent signals?
- If we introduce multi-language later: do we infer language from browser locale at signup or require explicit selection?

---

## Appendix: Reference links
- Messaging guidelines: Hosted API onboarding (Confluence)
- Lifecycle email + retargeting data handling policy (Confluence)
- Sprint brief: lifecycle growth experiments (Confluence)
- Console nudges experiment framework (Confluence)

- unsubscribed

We will report the onboarding funnel with breakdowns by:
- cohort (new/stalled/first success)
- variant (A/B)
- acquisition channel (UTM at signup)
- region

- A variant can “win” if it improves **first successful request within 7 days** by **≥ +3% relative** (or statistically credible lift) without violating deliverability guardrails.

- Primary window: 7 days post-signup.
- Retention check: D7 and D30 (directional within sprint; more confidence post-sprint).

---

1) Copy + design approved (Soojin + Design)
2) Compliance review completed (Michael + Olga)
3) Preference center link validated in console
4) Trigger logic QA:

5) UTM link audit
6) Test sends across Gmail/Outlook + mobile
7) Ramp plan configured + monitoring owner on-call for first 72 hours
8) Dashboard sanity checks (baseline vs. post-launch)

- Do we enable “reply-to monitored inbox” for E4 troubleshooting, or route to support form only?
- Should E6 include a lightweight “book a 15-min onboarding call” CTA, gated by upgrade intent signals?
- If we introduce multi-language later: do we infer language from browser locale at signup or require explicit selection?

- Messaging guidelines: Hosted API onboarding (Confluence)
- Lifecycle email + retargeting data handling policy (Confluence)
- Sprint brief: lifecycle growth experiments (Confluence)
- Console nudges experiment framework (Confluence)

## Overview
Email Onboarding Drip v2 is the primary lifecycle email sequence for new self-serve Hosted API signups. The sequence is designed to reduce time-to-first-token (TTFT), increase early activation (first successful request + first 100k tokens within 7 days), and improve D7/D30 return usage.

This document specifies triggers, segmentation, send schedule, copy variants, compliance requirements, deliverability guardrails, and the measurement / experiment plan.

**Primary audience:** Developers who sign up for Redwood API self-serve and have not yet reached early activation milestones.

**Owners**
- Experiment owner: Ben Carter (Growth/Marketing)
- Messaging review: Soojin Lee
- Marketing ops + attribution: Tyler Benson
- Telemetry + rollout input: Paula Mendes
- Legal/compliance review: Michael Grant (CAN-SPAM), Olga Petrov (GDPR)

---

## Goals and success metrics
### Goals
1) Increase activation within 7 days:
- **Activation milestone A:** “First successful request” (any successful completion from SDK/REST).
- **Activation milestone B:** “First 100k tokens” (cumulative) within 7 days of signup.

2) Reduce TTFT:
- Decrease median time from signup → first successful request.

3) Improve early retention:
- Increase D7 active usage and reduce “one-and-done” behavior.

### Success metrics (email + product)
**Email engagement (diagnostic):**
- Delivered rate
- Open rate (directional; impacted by privacy features)
- Click-through rate (CTR)
- Click-to-open rate (CTOR)
- Complaint rate, unsubscribe rate

**Activation / usage outcomes (decision metrics):**
- Signup → API key created conversion
- Signup → first successful request conversion
- Signup → first 100k tokens within 7 days conversion
- Median TTFT
- D7 active usage rate

**Guardrails:**
- Complaint rate < 0.08% weekly
- Unsubscribe rate < 1.0% per send (monitor trend)
- Hard bounce rate < 0.5%
- Support tickets for “not receiving onboarding email” should not exceed baseline +20% without investigation

---

## Non-goals / out of scope
- Pricing/packaging changes
- Major docs replatforming
- Complex personalization beyond core lifecycle events
- Advanced multi-language localization (v2 is English-only; revisit in Q2)

---

## Segmentation and eligibility
### Base eligibility
Users are eligible if:
- They create a Redwood account via self-serve Hosted API signup
- They have not opted out of marketing/lifecycle communications
- They are not suppressed by compliance or sales exclusions

### Suppression / exclusions
Suppress sends for:
1) **Unsubscribed users** (global marketing opt-out and/or lifecycle opt-out).
2) **Transactional-only preference** (if user selects that preference in console).
3) **Active enterprise opportunities / sensitive accounts** (to avoid conflicting outreach):
   - Domains / accounts on the Sales exclusion list (maintained weekly with Sales leadership).
   - Accounts flagged “Sales-assisted onboarding in progress” in CRM.
4) **Regulated / restricted tracking accounts** where policy requires reduced targeting (per data handling policy; see related page).

### Lifecycle cohorts (v2)
We define cohorts based on onboarding progress.

| Cohort | Definition (evaluated at send time) | Primary objective |
|---|---|---|
| New signup | Signed up within last 24h | Get to API key + first request |
| Stalled: no API key | Signup ≥ 2h ago AND no API key created | Push key creation and quickstart |
| Stalled: API key but no request | API key created AND no successful request | Get a working “hello world” call |
| First success (early) | First successful request completed AND tokens < 100k | Encourage streaming + dashboard + scale up |

**Note:** Cohorts are mutually exclusive at evaluation time; users can “graduate” to later cohorts and stop receiving earlier-stage messages.

---

## Event dependencies (triggers and state)
Email eligibility and stage progression are driven by product events.

### Required events (canonical names)
- `signup_completed`
- `api_key_created`
- `first_successful_request` (first time)
- `tokens_100k_reached` (first time)
- `email_unsubscribed` / `email_preferences_updated`

### Required user attributes (minimum)
- `user_id` (internal)
- `workspace_id` (if applicable)
- `primary_email`
- `signup_timestamp`
- `region` (for compliance + deliverability monitoring)
- `utm_source`, `utm_campaign` (if present at signup)

### Dedupe rules
- `first_successful_request` and `tokens_100k_reached` must be emitted once per user/workspace (per analytics spec).
- Email sends are idempotent by `(user_id, email_template_id, stage_id)`.

---

## Trigger logic and send schedule
This is a staged drip with time-based triggers and event-based exits.

### High-level sequence
1) **Welcome + Quickstart** (T+15 min after signup)
2) **No API key reminder** (T+6h if no API key)
3) **Make your first request** (T+24h if API key but no first success)
4) **Streaming + reliability tips** (T+48h if still no first success OR low engagement)
5) **After first success: scale to 100k tokens** (event-based; T+2h after first success)
6) **D7 check-in** (T+7d after signup; conditional)

### Detailed schedule and gating
| Email | Template ID | Trigger | Send window | Exit condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| E1: Welcome + quickstart | `onboard_v2_e1` | `signup_completed` | 15 min after signup | Stop if user unsubscribes |
| E2: Create an API key | `onboard_v2_e2` | Time-based check | 6h after signup | Do not send if `api_key_created` already occurred |
| E3: First request (hello world) | `onboard_v2_e3` | Time-based check | 24h after signup | Do not send if `first_successful_request` already occurred |
| E4: Debug + common pitfalls | `onboard_v2_e4` | Time-based check | 48h after signup | Do not send if `first_successful_request` already occurred |
| E5: After first success | `onboard_v2_e5` | `first_successful_request` | 2h after first success | Do not send if `tokens_100k_reached` already occurred |
| E6: D7 check-in | `onboard_v2_e6` | Time-based check | 7d after signup | Suppress if user has been active in last 48h AND has reached 100k tokens |

### Quiet hours + throttling
- **Default send window:** 08:00–17:00 recipient local time when possible (fallback to account region).
- **Deliverability ramp:** Start at 10% of eligible users/day for 2 days, then 25%, then 50%, then 100% if guardrails hold.

---

## Messaging framework (what we emphasize)
We align with Hosted API onboarding messaging guidelines:
- **Developer-first:** clear steps, minimal jargon, copy that assumes “I just want my first request to work.”
- **Production outcomes:** low-latency inference, predictable cost, reliability/fallbacks, and easy path from prototype to production.
- **Avoid overpromising:** no absolute latency guarantees in lifecycle email; point to benchmarks/docs when needed.

Key proof points to weave in (lightweight):
- Streaming support
- Clear unit economics controls (batching/caching/quantization—only mention if relevant to stage)
- Observability (usage dashboard and token-level cost breakdown)

---

Each email is assembled from standardized modules to keep design consistent and reduce iteration time.

**Shared modules**
- Header: Redwood Inference (simple, dark-on-light)
- One-sentence value prop (stage-specific)
- Primary CTA button
- Code block / snippet (where applicable)
- “Need help?” link (docs + support)
- Footer: address, unsubscribe, preferences

**Design constraints**
- Mobile-first; keep above-the-fold to a single CTA + 1–2 short paragraphs.
- Code blocks must wrap gracefully; use short lines and avoid wide tables.

source.author: Ben Carter
source.body:
## Purpose
This playbook defines how AEs/SDRs/SEs/CSMs should follow up on product-led growth (PLG) signals that indicate a self-serve customer is a strong candidate for a sales-assisted upgrade to **Redwood Dedicated** or **Redwood Private**.

The goal is to:
- Respond quickly to real need (scale, reliability, security/compliance) without spamming customers.
- Standardize messaging and discovery so we qualify consistently.
- Ensure clean handoffs to SE and CS and consistent outcome logging for attribution.

**Pilot scope (Phase 2):** NA self-serve cohort only; HubSpot task-based execution (no automated sequencing beyond task templates).

---

## Quick start (what to do when you get a PLG Signal task)
1) **Open the HubSpot Company record → “PLG Signals” section** and read:
   - Latest signal type(s) and score
   - The “Why routed” summary (top contributing metrics)
   - Suppression history (to avoid duplicate touches)

2) **Determine motion (choose one):**
   - **Scale motion (Dedicated)**: sustained usage/spend growth, multi-workspace/team expansion
   - **Reliability motion (Dedicated)**: repeated 429s/timeouts/latency regressions, SLO risk
   - **Compliance motion (Private or Dedicated+security add-ons)**: security doc intent, residency, VPC/on-prem interest

3) **Check guardrails before outreach** (see “Outreach guardrails & suppression” below).

4) **Send the Day 0 email + optionally call** if signal is high confidence (see scoring guidance). If compliance-driven, loop SE early.

5) **Log outcomes** in HubSpot within 24 hours (Meeting booked / Nurture / Disqualified / Already engaged / Existing customer managed by CS).

---

## Signal taxonomy (what counts, and what we do)
Signals are grouped into two categories:
- **Threshold signals (behavior at scale):** quantitative usage/spend/error thresholds.
- **Intent signals (stated interest):** pricing, Dedicated/Private docs, security content consumption, residency/VPC/on-prem exploration.

### Canonical signal types and recommended motions
**A) Scale (upgrade to Dedicated)**
Trigger examples (any one can qualify; multiple raise priority):
- Sustained token volume growth over a 7–14 day window.
- Spend velocity increasing week-over-week.
- Multi-workspace creation or rapid org member invites (team expansion).
- High throughput embeddings usage with consistent daily load (predictable reserved capacity fit).

Recommended motion:
- Primary offer: **Dedicated** (predictable throughput + isolation + cost control).
- Secondary: keep Hosted with optimization guidance if customer is early-stage / spiky traffic.

**B) Reliability (stabilize + Dedicated)**
Trigger examples:
- Repeated **429 (rate-limit)** responses over multiple days.
- Elevated p95/p99 latency vs baseline for that model route.
- Increased retries/timeouts that correlate with production errors.

Recommended motion:
- Primary offer: **Dedicated** (capacity guarantees + routing control + higher limits).
- Secondary: short-term mitigations (quota adjustments, batching/caching guidance) with a clear path to Dedicated.

**C) Compliance / Security (Private or Dedicated with enterprise controls)**
Trigger examples:
- Security FAQ / SOC2 / DPA / audit log documentation views.
- VPC / Private deployment docs views.
- EU data residency signals or region-specific concerns.
- Evidence request language in inbound messages or support tickets.

Recommended motion:
- Primary offer: **Private** for VPC/on-prem, strict residency, or regulated workloads.
- Alternative: **Dedicated** when the need is isolation + SLA but not full private control plane.

---

## Scoring + prioritization (how to triage)
Signals are routed with an internal score (0–100) based on volume, duration, and intent strength. Use this to decide whether to call immediately or start with email.

**Priority bands**
- **P0 (Score 80–100):** Contact same business day. If compliance-driven, schedule SE within 72 hours.
- **P1 (Score 60–79):** Day 0 email; call within 48 hours.
- **P2 (Score 40–59):** Email only; monitor; convert to nurture if no reply.
- **Below 40:** No direct outreach unless additional context exists (inbound request, support escalation, known ICP).

**Important:** The score is a guide. If the company is already in a live sales cycle or is owned by CS, follow the ownership/handoff rules.

---

## Outreach guardrails & suppression (do not spam)
Before reaching out, confirm:
- **No recent sales outreach:** If any AE/SDR touch occurred in the last **14 days**, do not re-trigger without a materially new signal.
- **Marketing nurture conflict:** If the customer is in a high-touch lifecycle campaign, coordinate with Marketing Ops (Tyler Benson) or apply the “sales-first” suppression rule for P0/P1 signals.
- **Existing customer expansion:** If there is an assigned CSM (Customer Success), notify CS and coordinate a joint plan (see handoff section). Do not run an SDR cadence that conflicts with QBR/renewal.
- **Customer complaint history:** If the account previously reported unwanted outreach, require AE manager approval before re-contacting.
- **Data minimization:** Do not reference precise internal telemetry (“we saw X tokens/day”) in outbound. Use customer-centric language (“teams often hit scaling limits at this stage”).

---

## Messaging framework (by signal type)
Use the same structure across all motions:
1) Observation (non-creepy) + hypothesis
2) Value proposition
3) Ask for a short working session
4) Clear agenda

### A) Scale messaging (Hosted → Dedicated)
**What we’re trying to learn:** Is their usage stable/predictable enough for Dedicated, and are cost/latency/throughput constraints becoming product constraints?

**Email opener examples**
- “Noticed your team is scaling Redwood usage—wanted to share a few patterns we see when teams move from self-serve to a more predictable capacity setup.”
- “If you’re planning to expand usage beyond a single team/workspace, we can help you lock in throughput and simplify rollout and governance.”

**Value props to emphasize**
- Predictable throughput and latency targets
- Higher limits + reduced throttling risk
- Better unit economics via batching/caching + reserved capacity
- Isolation and control (without running your own inference stack)

**CTA**
- “Open to a 20-minute working session this week? We can map your workload (peak TPS, seq lengths, model mix) to a Dedicated configuration and pricing range.”

### B) Reliability messaging (429s/latency/timeouts)

- Multi-workspace creation or rapid org member invites (team expansion).
- High throughput embeddings usage with consistent daily load (predictable reserved capacity fit).

Recommended motion:
- Primary offer: **Dedicated** (predictable throughput + isolation + cost control).
- Secondary: keep Hosted with optimization guidance if customer is early-stage / spiky traffic.

**B) Reliability (stabilize + Dedicated)**
Trigger examples:
- Repeated **429 (rate-limit)** responses over multiple days.
- Elevated p95/p99 latency vs baseline for that model route.
- Increased retries/timeouts that correlate with production errors.

- Primary offer: **Dedicated** (capacity guarantees + routing control + higher limits).

**C) Compliance / Security (Private or Dedicated with enterprise controls)**

- Security FAQ / SOC2 / DPA / audit log documentation views.
- VPC / Private deployment docs views.

**What we’re trying to learn:** Is their usage stable/predictable enough for Dedicated, and are cost/latency/throughput constraints becoming product constraints?

**Email opener examples**
- “Noticed your team is scaling Redwood usage—wanted to share a few patterns we see when teams move from self-serve to a more predictable capacity setup.”
- “If you’re planning to expand usage beyond a single team/workspace, we can help you lock in throughput and simplify rollout and governance.”

**Value props to emphasize**
- Predictable throughput and latency targets
- Higher limits + reduced throttling risk
- Better unit economics via batching/caching + reserved capacity
- Isolation and control (without running your own inference stack)

**CTA**
- “Open to a 20-minute working session this week? We can map your workload (peak TPS, seq lengths, model mix) to a Dedicated configuration and pricing range.”

### B) Reliability messaging (429s/latency/timeouts)
**What we’re trying to learn:** Are reliability issues impacting production SLAs and customer experience? Is Dedicated the clean fix vs tuning quotas/rate limits?

**Email opener examples**
- “A few teams reach a point where self-serve limits and bursty traffic create reliability headaches (throttling, retries). If that’s happening, we can help stabilize quickly.”
- “If latency and throttling are starting to affect product experience, we can review options to get consistent headroom.”

**Value props to emphasize**
- Capacity headroom and dedicated routing
- SLA-backed uptime/latency options
- Clear escalation path + operational tooling (dashboards, rollouts, fallbacks)

**CTA**
- “Can we do a quick reliability review? We’ll cover current limits, traffic patterns, and what ‘good’ looks like for p95/p99.”

### C) Compliance/security messaging (Dedicated/Private)
**What we’re trying to learn:** What specific control is required (SSO/SAML, audit logs, retention, residency, VPC/on-prem), and what timeline is driving it (procurement, customer demand, regulatory milestone)?

**Email opener examples**
- “If you’re evaluating security/compliance requirements for expanding LLM usage, we can walk through Redwood’s controls (audit logs, retention, key management, deployment options).”
- “Some teams need VPC/private deployment and stronger governance as they move into regulated workflows—happy to compare options and share evidence artifacts.”

**Value props to emphasize**
- Private deployments (VPC/on-prem options)
- Audit logs, retention controls, KMS/HSM integrations
- Data residency / region routing options
- Security review process + evidence package readiness

**CTA**
- “If helpful, we can schedule a 30-minute security overview with an SE and share the relevant SOC2/audit materials under NDA.”

---

## Discovery questions (use in first meeting)
Aim for a tight discovery to validate fit and route to the right SKU.

### Universal (ask every time)
- What user experience are you protecting? (latency, reliability, output quality)
- What’s the business-critical workflow? (support agents, search, content generation, internal tooling)
- What models/routes are you using today? Any planned changes?
- What’s the traffic pattern: steady vs bursty; time-of-day peaks; region distribution?
- What are the top constraints right now: cost, limits, latency, governance, procurement?

### Scale-specific
- How many teams/workspaces will be using this in the next 90 days?
- Are you optimizing for lowest cost per token or for consistent latency under peak load?
- What are success metrics for an upgrade? (throughput, p95 latency, max concurrency, unit cost)

### Reliability-specific
- Where do errors show up (429, timeouts, internal app retries)? How does it impact end users?
- What’s your current retry/backoff strategy?
- Do you need per-route guarantees or global org-level capacity?

### Compliance/security-specific
- Which requirement is driving this? (SOC2, HIPAA, GDPR, internal policy, customer questionnaire)
- Do you need VPC, on-prem, or is dedicated isolation sufficient?
- Data retention needs: default, maximum retention, deletion requests.
- AuthN/AuthZ requirements: SSO/SAML, SCIM, role-based access.
- Evidence timeline: when do you need a security review completed?

---

## Meeting goals + next steps (what “good” looks like)
### First call goals (20–30 minutes)
- Confirm the trigger problem (scale/reliability/compliance) in the customer’s words.
- Identify the correct motion (Dedicated vs Private vs optimize self-serve).
- Secure agreement on a concrete next step:
  - **Dedicated:** benchmark plan + sizing inputs + pricing range
  - **Private:** security review + deployment requirements checklist
  - **Not ready:** nurture plan with explicit re-check timing

### Outputs to capture (in HubSpot)
- Workload summary: model mix, peak concurrency, regions, critical latency target.
- Constraints: compliance checkboxes, procurement timeline, budget signals.
- Stakeholders: Eng owner, security owner, procurement/legal.
- Next meeting date + owner.

---

## Handoff points (when to loop in SE/CS/Security)
### When to involve an SE (Markus Klein’s team)
Loop in Solutions Engineering immediately if any of the following are true:
- Private/VPC/on-prem interest signals or explicit questions.
- Security/compliance discovery is non-trivial (audit logs, KMS/HSM, residency commitments, DPA redlines).
- Reliability issues are production-impacting and require architecture guidance.
- Customer requests benchmarks, performance proof, or a mutual action plan.

**SE handoff package (paste into internal notes):**
- Signal type + short context
- Current usage pattern (qualitative; avoid exact telemetry in shared customer channels)
- Customer objective and deadline
- Known blockers + stakeholders

### When to involve Customer Success (Jasmine Liu)
Notify CS and coordinate outreach when:
- The account is already a paying customer with a CSM assigned.
- The signal indicates multi-team rollout or expansion inside an existing org.
- The customer’s message reads like “support escalation” rather than “sales interest.”

**CS coordination rule:** If there is a CSM, the CSM owns the primary relationship. AE should align on joint messaging and avoid parallel cadences.

### When to involve Security/Compliance
Engage Security/Compliance reviewers (Dr. Aisha Rahman / Marcus Lin / Olga Petrov) when:
- Customer requests specific evidence artifacts beyond the standard package.
- There are atypical data handling requirements (custom retention, data residency, audit log export constraints).
- There is an escalation risk (regulated healthcare/fintech + tight timelines).

---

## Cadence (tasks + timing)
Use HubSpot task templates (do not freestyle high-volume outreach during pilot).

### Standard cadence (P0/P1)
- **Day 0:** Email + (optional) call
- **Day 2:** Follow-up email with a specific agenda and two time options
- **Day 5:** Short call attempt + concise email (“close the loop”)
- **Day 10:** Final check-in + offer to route to self-serve optimization resources

**Exit criteria (stop the cadence):**
- Meeting booked
- Customer replies “not interested” / “stop”
- Disqualified (student project, internal/test, non-ICP)
- Ownership is reassigned to CS or active opportunity exists

### Nurture cadence (P2)
- Single email with a helpful resource (Dedicated overview, scaling guide, security FAQ)
- Set a reminder in 30 days to re-check signals

---

## Objections + recommended responses
**“We’re fine on self-serve.”**
- “Totally—many teams stay on self-serve. The main reason teams switch is when throughput and reliability start to become product constraints. If it’s helpful, we can sanity-check future growth and share what thresholds typically change the economics.”

**“We don’t want sales outreach.”**
- “Understood. We reached out because teams at your stage sometimes want help avoiding scaling issues. I can close the loop and only re-engage if you request it. If you’d like, I can share one resource and step back.” (Then mark suppression / opt-out clearly.)

**“We need enterprise security controls.”**
- “We can help. The fastest path is a short security overview to map which controls you need (SSO, audit logs, retention, residency, VPC). Then we’ll recommend either Dedicated with the right controls or Private deployment.”

**“Pricing?”**
- “We can share a range once we understand workload shape (model mix, peak concurrency, seq lengths). Dedicated is usually driven by capacity and SLO needs. If you share a few parameters, we can come back with a sizing + pricing estimate and options.”

---

## How to log outcomes (required)
In HubSpot, update the latest PLG Signal task with one outcome:
- **Contacted** (email/call completed)
- **Meeting booked** (add meeting link)
- **Evaluation started** (POC defined)
- **Opportunity created** (Dedicated/Private)
- **Nurture** (set follow-up reminder)
- **Disqualified** (include reason)
- **False positive / Suppress** (include the reason and recommended rule)

This logging is required for attribution and for improving suppression/noise rules.

---

## Privacy and “don’t be creepy” guidelines
- Do not cite exact internal metrics (tokens/day, spend) in outbound.
- Avoid “we saw you reading X page at Y time.” You may reference broad categories (“security documentation” / “deployment options”).
- If asked how we knew, say: “We use aggregated, privacy-aware product signals to offer help when teams may be running into scaling or compliance requirements. We can stop these prompts if you prefer.”

For data handling details, see: **PLG Signal Data Handling and Retention**.

---

## FAQ
**Q: Who owns the account if it’s unowned in HubSpot?**
A: Ownership is assigned via region/tier rules with round-robin fallback (see routing ADR). If you believe ownership is wrong, tag Sales Ops (Isabelle Laurent) and do not outreach until resolved.

**Q: What if the signal is clearly a support issue (errors/429s) and customer is angry?**
A: Treat it as support first. Loop in CS/Support and offer an SE reliability review; do not pitch upgrades in the first response.

**Q: What if the customer already opted out of sales outreach?**
A: Respect the opt-out. Mark the signal as suppressed and coordinate with CS for relationship-safe engagement only if requested.

---

## Change log
- 2025-06-12: Added stricter suppression guidance (14-day window) and clarified CS ownership for existing customers.
- 2025-05-22: Updated compliance messaging to explicitly offer SE-led security overview.
- 2025-05-08: Initial published version for NA pilot.

source.title: Upgrade triggers: Hosted → Dedicated / Private (pricing + packaging guidance)
source.author: Ben Carter
source.content:
## Purpose
This page provides clear guidance for when to recommend an upgrade from **Redwood Hosted API** to **Redwood Dedicated** or **Redwood Private**, based on usage thresholds and common pain signals (latency, rate limits, reliability, compliance). It also includes **pricing/packaging guardrails** and approved **discount/credit escalation paths**.

This doc is used by AEs, SDRs, SEs, and CSMs during the PLG-to-sales motion. It is designed to reduce inconsistent recommendations and prevent over-discounting or pushing Dedicated/Private when Hosted is still the right fit.

## Quick decision tree (first pass)
Use this as the default mapping before deep discovery.

1) **Compliance / data controls required?**
- If the customer requires any of the following as a gating requirement: **VPC-only networking**, **customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM)**, **custom retention/audit evidence**, **data residency controls**, **on-prem/air-gapped**, or **no shared infrastructure** → start with **Private** (or Dedicated + add-ons only if it meets requirements).

2) **Scale / throughput predictability required?**
- If usage is high and/or spiky, and they need predictable throughput, isolation, or higher rate limits → start with **Dedicated**.

3) **Neither compliance nor scale is gating**
- Keep them on **Hosted** and optimize (batching, caching, model choice, quotas). Only upgrade if pain persists after optimization.

## Canonical “upgrade signals” (what counts)
Signals fall into two categories:
- **Threshold signals**: quantitative and sustained (usage/spend, rate limiting, error rates).
- **Intent/pain signals**: qualitative or behavior-based (Dedicated/Private docs interest, repeated security FAQ views, explicit asks for isolation/SLA, procurement cues).

For PLG signal routing, we treat upgrades as “sales-worthy” when we see:
- **Sustained thresholds** (not single-day spikes) AND/OR
- **High-intent compliance behaviors** (security + deployment docs) AND/OR
- **Recurring reliability/429 pain** that impacts production.

## Recommended upgrade thresholds (guidance, not a contract)
All thresholds should be evaluated over a **rolling 7-day and 30-day window** to reduce false positives.

### A) Hosted → Dedicated: usage/scale triggers
Recommend Dedicated when one or more of the following are true (especially if paired with reliability pain):

- **Spend velocity**: $3,000+ in the last 30 days OR projected to exceed $5,000/month within the next 30 days based on trailing 7-day trend.
- **Sustained token volume**: 150M+ tokens in last 30 days OR 5M+ tokens/day sustained for 7 consecutive days.
- **Consistent throttling**: 429 rate-limit responses on 3+ days in a 7-day window, or customer reports rate limiting is blocking a launch.
- **Throughput needs**: explicit requirement for stable throughput (e.g., “we need X req/s” or “we need predictable tokens/sec”) or business-critical production workloads with measurable SLOs.
- **Org expansion**: multiple workspaces/teams created and usage distributed across teams (often correlates with upcoming enterprise asks like consolidated billing, RBAC, audit logs).

### B) Hosted → Private: compliance/deployment triggers
Recommend Private when compliance, network, or residency requirements are gating.

- **VPC / private networking**: customer requires requests to stay inside their VPC, private connectivity, or restricted egress.
- **Key management**: requirement for customer-managed keys (KMS/HSM), key rotation policies, or encryption evidence beyond Hosted guarantees.
- **Audit & evidence**: needs detailed audit logs, evidence packages, or retention controls tied to internal policies.
- **Data residency**: explicit EU-only or region-pinned processing requirement that cannot be satisfied with Hosted region options.
- **Regulated workload**: healthcare/financial services/legal with DPA/security review as a formal gating step.
- **On-prem / air-gapped**: any mention of air-gap, on-prem, or restricted environment → Private.

### C) “Not yet” guidance (stay on Hosted)
Do NOT push upgrades when:
- Usage is below the Dedicated threshold bands AND there is no sustained pain signal.
- There is a single-day spike (launch/test) without sustained growth.
- The customer’s issue is model selection/prompting quality, not serving constraints.
- The customer’s complaint is primarily cost but they are not yet at scale; prioritize **Hosted Optimize** recommendations first.

## Pain signal → recommendation mapping
Use this table during discovery and in follow-up emails.

**Signal: Repeated 429s / throttling**
- Likely cause: shared capacity, quota mismatch, bursty traffic
- Recommended action: tune quotas + batching; if recurring in prod → Dedicated
- Notes: ask for peak traffic shape and launch timeline

**Signal: Latency sensitivity / p95 targets**
- Likely cause: shared fleet variability, long context, model choice
- Recommended action: benchmark on Hosted with tuning; if strict p95 SLO and production-critical → Dedicated
- Notes: confirm p50/p95 goals and max context lengths

**Signal: Need for isolation (data or performance)**
- Likely cause: internal policy or multi-tenant concerns
- Recommended action: Dedicated (performance isolation) or Private (network/compliance isolation)
- Notes: clarify if they mean “no shared GPUs” vs “no public internet”

**Signal: Security/compliance review initiated**
- Likely cause: procurement readiness, regulated environment
- Recommended action: route to SE + consider Private; Dedicated may be acceptable with enterprise security add-ons depending on requirements
- Notes: use the Security FAQ and capture evidence checklist

**Signal: “We need a custom SLA”**
- Recommended action: Dedicated or Private depending on deployment requirements
- Notes: loop Sales Ops early for SLA language and feasibility

**Signal: Procurement/legal asks (DPA, retention controls)**
- Recommended action: start enterprise motion; Private if retention/network is gating
- Notes: involve Legal/Compliance early; avoid promising timelines without review

## Qualification checklist (ask these before recommending)
Capture these in HubSpot notes and use them to route to SE/CS appropriately.

1) Workload
- Primary endpoints: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank
- Peak vs average traffic (req/s, tokens/sec)
- Context length distribution (median, p95)
- Streaming required?

2) Reliability
- Current p50/p95 latency and error rates
- Any SLOs (internal or customer-facing)?
- Is this production, beta, or internal tool?

3) Security/compliance
- Data classification (PII/PHI/PCI)
- Residency requirements
- Network requirements (public internet ok? private link?)
- Key management requirements (customer-managed keys?)

4) Commercial
- Current spend and growth trajectory
- Timeline (launch date, procurement deadline)
- Buying center (engineering-led vs security/procurement-led)

## Packaging guidance (what we’re allowed to promise)
### Hosted API
Best for: fast onboarding, variable workloads, smaller teams, non-gated compliance.

Allowed commitments:
- Standard Hosted uptime/availability statements (per current standard terms)
- Best-effort quota increases when supported by capacity

Avoid:
- Committing to dedicated GPUs or strict p95 SLAs on Hosted
- Promising “no noisy neighbor” guarantees

### Dedicated
Best for: predictable throughput, high volume, performance isolation, SLA-backed operations.

Positioning:
- Dedicated GPU pools for predictable tokens/sec and isolation
- Better controls for autoscaling and burst capacity

Guardrails:
- Dedicated is not automatically “compliance complete.” If they need VPC-only or strict residency, evaluate Private.
- Any SLA language must use the approved template (Sales Ops).

### Private
Best for: regulated workloads, VPC/on-prem constraints, custom retention/audit, customer-managed keys.

Guardrails:
- Private deals require early Security/Compliance review (do not delay; it often dictates timeline).
- Do not promise air-gapped or on-prem delivery dates without Platform confirmation.

## Pricing guidance (high-level)
This section is intentionally directional; use the current pricing sheet and Sales Ops for exact numbers.

### When to frame Dedicated as an economic win
Dedicated is easiest to justify when customers have:
- Sustained volume where unit economics improve with reserved capacity
- Production requirements where downtime/latency variability has real business cost

Messaging:
- “Dedicated reduces the probability of throttling and gives you predictable throughput.”
- “We can align cost to your usage profile with reserved capacity and scaling policies.”

### When to frame Private as risk reduction
Private is justified when:
- Compliance is gating and the alternative is losing the deal or delaying launch
- Security requirements require stronger isolation/evidence

Messaging:
- “Private is about meeting your network, residency, and audit requirements so you can ship confidently.”

## Discounting + credits (guardrails and escalation)
We use discounts/credits to accelerate evaluation and reduce friction, not to buy deals prematurely.

### Allowed without escalation (AE discretion within pilot guardrails)
- **One-time evaluation credits** for a time-boxed POC when a customer is at/near a Dedicated trigger and has a clear success plan.
- **Short-term Hosted credits** to unblock a launch while Dedicated/Private is being scoped (must be paired with a scheduled follow-up).

Documentation required:
- Success criteria (latency/throughput targets)
- POC timeline (start/end)
- Exit criteria (what converts them)

### Requires Sales Ops approval (Isabelle Laurent) + CRO visibility
- Any discount beyond the standard band in the pricing sheet
- Multi-quarter credit programs
- Retroactive credits to offset past Hosted overages

### Requires Security/Compliance sign-off before commercial commitments
- Any Private deployment with customer-managed keys, custom retention, residency constraints, or regulated workload claims

### Customer complaint / “unwanted outreach” sensitivity
If the customer indicates outreach was unexpected or unwelcome:
- Stop cadence immediately
- Apologize and clarify intent (“supporting scale/security needs”) without referencing internal signal details
- Add them to the appropriate suppression list per the PLG signal process
- If needed, coordinate with Support (SUP ticket) and Marketing Ops to avoid duplicate touches

## Recommended next steps by scenario
### Scenario 1: High growth + recurring 429s (Dedicated)
- Action: schedule a 30-min scale review + SE benchmark plan
- Deliverable: proposed capacity plan + expected throughput + Dedicated overview
- Exit: customer agrees to a 2-week POC on Dedicated or signs reserved capacity

### Scenario 2: Security FAQ + VPC docs interest (Private)
- Action: schedule security review with SE + Security/Compliance
- Deliverable: evidence checklist + deployment options overview + data handling summary
- Exit: mutual action plan for Private evaluation and procurement timeline

### Scenario 3: Latency complaints but low spend (Hosted optimize first)
- Action: run optimization checklist (batching/caching/model choice) and measure improvement
- Deliverable: before/after benchmark and recommended config
- Exit: if p95 targets remain unmet and workload is production-critical → Dedicated

## HubSpot notes and attribution requirements
To help measure the PLG signal motion, log these outcomes on the Company record:
- Contacted (yes/no + date)
- Meeting booked
- Evaluation started (Dedicated/Private)
- Opportunity created (Dedicated/Private)
- Disqualified (reason)

Also tag:
- Primary driver: Scale / Reliability / Compliance / Procurement
- Recommended path: Dedicated / Private / Stay Hosted

## FAQ
**Q: If a customer asks for an SLA, is Dedicated mandatory?**
A: Usually, yes. Hosted cannot guarantee strict p95 latency or dedicated throughput. Route to Dedicated unless their “SLA” request is informal and solvable via configuration.

**Q: A customer wants “dedicated” but also says “VPC only.” Which is it?**
A: Treat it as Private. Dedicated addresses performance isolation; VPC-only is a deployment/network requirement.

**Q: Can we offer large credits to move them faster?**
A: Not without Sales Ops approval and a documented POC plan. Credits must be time-boxed and tied to success criteria.

**Q: What if we see intent signals (docs views) but no usage growth yet?**
A: Do light-touch outreach and qualify. Do not over-rotate on docs views alone; confirm a real timeline and gating requirement.

## Ownership and escalation
- Pricing exceptions / discount approvals: **Isabelle Laurent (Sales Ops)**
- HubSpot properties/workflows questions: **David Kim (Biz Systems)**
- Private deployment feasibility: **Priya Natarajan (Platform)** + **Markus Klein (SE)**
- Security/compliance review: **Dr. Aisha Rahman** / **Marcus Lin** / **Olga Petrov**
- Pilot process issues and playbook updates: **Ben Carter** + **Stephanie Nguyen**

## Change log
- 2025-06-07: Clarified Dedicated vs Private decision tree; added discount escalation language; aligned outcome logging fields with PLG signal routing pilot.
- 2025-05-12: Initial publication for NA pilot.

## Discounting + credits (guardrails and escalation)

- Stop cadence immediately
